title: Designing and Implementing Cloud-Native Applications Using Microsoft Azure Cosmos DB
passing_note: DP-420 passes at 700/1000. Microsoft does not publish the raw-score mapping, so the readiness estimate below is unofficial and based on percent correct only.

readiness_bands.label: Ready
readiness_bands.note: Consistently strong — you're tracking well above the pass line.

readiness_bands.label: Approaching
readiness_bands.note: Close — shore up weaker domains before test day.

readiness_bands.label: Not ready yet
readiness_bands.note: Keep studying — focus on your lowest-scoring domains.

scenarios.s-social: Contoso Social runs a high-traffic social feed on Azure Cosmos DB for NoSQL. Users create short posts; each post can accumulate an unbounded number of comments and reactions over time. The most frequent operation by far is loading a single user's profile page with their most recent posts. The team wants cheap, single-partition reads for that page and predictable cost as the app grows.
scenarios.s-retail: Fabrikam Retail stores orders, order line items, and product catalog entries in Azure Cosmos DB for NoSQL. Orders are written once and rarely change; catalog entries are read constantly but updated occasionally. Reporting queries frequently read an order together with all of its line items, and the checkout service must update an order and its inventory record atomically when they share a partition key value.
scenarios.s-global: Tailwind Global serves customers on three continents from a single Azure Cosmos DB for NoSQL account. Leadership wants the lowest possible read and write latency for users in every region and the ability to keep operating if one Azure region becomes unavailable. Occasional conflicting writes to the same item from different regions are acceptable as long as they are resolved deterministically.
scenarios.s-iot: Adatum IoT ingests millions of device telemetry events per hour into Azure Cosmos DB for NoSQL. A downstream service must react to every new or changed item in near real time to update aggregates and push alerts, and the analytics team needs to run large historical aggregations without affecting the write throughput of the transactional workload.
scenarios.s-fin: Woodgrove Bank operates a regulated workload on Azure Cosmos DB for NoSQL. Auditors require point-in-time restore to recover from accidental deletes, least-privilege access with no account keys in application code, network isolation from the public internet, and encryption using keys the bank controls. The operations team manages everything through declarative infrastructure-as-code.

domains.title: Integrate an Azure Cosmos DB solution
Optimize an Azure Cosmos DB solution
Maintain an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: A container holds both book and review items. How does application code tell one entity type from another when reading them?

options.text: By the item's partition key path
By a discriminator property such as type that each item carries
By the container's unique key policy
By storing each type in a separate database

questions.explanation: Cosmos DB containers are schema-agnostic, so applications rely on a discriminator property (commonly type) to branch logic and filter queries. The partition key and unique key policy are separate concepts and are not used to identify entity shape.
questions.domain_title: Design and implement data models

questions.stem: An order always displays a handful of line items together with the order, and the list never grows large. What is the best modeling choice?

options.text: Store each line item in its own container
Reference the line items from a second container synced by the change feed
Embed the line items inside the order document
Store the line items in a separate database

questions.explanation: Bounded, one-to-few data that is always read together should be embedded so a single point read returns everything. Referencing or splitting into separate containers would add extra reads for no benefit here.
questions.domain_title: Design and implement data models

questions.stem: Which conditions favor embedding related data in a single item? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: The data is read together in the same operation
The relationship is one-to-few and bounded
The child collection grows without limit
The related data changes together
The child list is very large and written independently

questions.explanation: Embed for contained one-to-few relationships, data that changes together, and data read together. Unbounded growth or very large, independently written child lists should be referenced because an item has a hard 2 MB limit.
questions.domain_title: Design and implement data models

questions.stem: A book listing shows the author's name on every card, and author names almost never change. What denormalization improves read performance while staying correct?

options.text: Reference the author and join at read time
Enforce a foreign key from book to author
Copy authorName into each book and update the copies via the change feed on the rare rename
Store author names in a separate database and query both

questions.explanation: Duplicating a few stable, read-together fields (like authorName) removes a second lookup; the change feed keeps the copies in sync when the source of truth changes. Cosmos DB has no joins or foreign keys.
questions.domain_title: Design and implement data models

questions.stem: Each publisher has thousands of books, and book records change often. Should a book embed its publisher or reference it?

options.text: Reference the publisher by storing a publisherId
Embed the full publisher document in every book
Duplicate every publisher field into each book
Add a unique key on the publisher name

questions.explanation: One-to-many-unbounded, frequently changing data should be referenced. Embedding or duplicating the publisher would bloat items and force volatile data to be kept in sync across thousands of books.
questions.domain_title: Design and implement data models

questions.stem: Which situations call for referencing (normalization) between documents rather than embedding? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: A one-to-many relationship that is unbounded
A many-to-many relationship
Small, stable data that is always read together
Related data that changes frequently or is large

questions.explanation: Reference for unbounded one-to-many, many-to-many, and frequently changing or large related data. Small, stable, read-together data is the classic case for embedding instead.
questions.domain_title: Design and implement data models

questions.stem: In a referenced design, who ensures that a stored publisherId actually points to a publisher that exists?

options.text: Cosmos DB foreign-key constraints
The container's unique key policy
Automatic cascading deletes
The application, optionally aided by triggers or change-feed processing

questions.explanation: Cosmos DB enforces no foreign keys or referential integrity, so maintaining valid references is the application's responsibility (optionally with triggers or the change feed). There are no cascading deletes.
questions.domain_title: Design and implement data models

questions.stem: A developer wants to change an item's partition key value after it was inserted. What must they do?

options.text: Update the item's partition key property in place
Use a patch operation to replace the partition key path
Delete the item and recreate it with the new partition key value
Alter the container's partition key definition

questions.explanation: The partition key value is immutable once an item exists, so the only way to "change" it is to delete and recreate the item. The container's partition key path also cannot be altered after creation.
questions.domain_title: Design and implement data models

questions.stem: Which statements about id and unique keys in Cosmos DB are true? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: Unique keys are enforced within a single logical partition, not globally
Unique keys can be added or changed at any time after the container exists
Unique keys are defined only at container creation
id is case-sensitive and unique within a logical partition

questions.explanation: Uniqueness (both id and unique keys) is scoped to a logical partition, and unique-key policies are immutable, set only at container creation. So the same value can repeat under a different partition key value.
questions.domain_title: Design and implement data models

questions.stem: Before choosing embed vs. reference and a partition key, what should you enumerate first?

options.text: A fully normalized entity-relationship diagram
The application's read and write access patterns
The container's indexing policy
The account's default consistency level

questions.explanation: Cosmos DB modeling is access-pattern driven: list the queries and mutations (frequency, latency, volume) first, then shape items and pick a partition key to serve the top ones as cheap single-partition operations. You do not start from a normalized ER diagram.
questions.domain_title: Design and implement data models

questions.stem: You want most items to live forever, but a few to self-expire using their own ttl. What container DefaultTimeToLive value do you set?

options.text: null
0
-1
A large positive number of seconds

questions.explanation: -1 enables TTL with no blanket expiry, so only items that set their own ttl expire. null disables TTL entirely (item ttl is ignored), and a positive value would expire everything by default.
questions.domain_title: Design and implement data models

questions.stem: Document schema versioning (tracking an item's shape with a schemaVersion property) is the same thing as document versioning (keeping a history of an entity's data).
questions.explanation: They are different concerns: schema versioning tracks how to interpret an item's shape as it evolves, while document versioning preserves a history of an entity's data by storing prior states as separate items.

options.text: True
False

questions.domain_title: Design and implement data models

questions.stem: A single partition key value is projected to store 35 GB. What limit does this violate, and what should change?

options.text: The 2 MB item limit; split the item into smaller documents
The 20 GB logical-partition limit; choose a higher-cardinality, synthetic, or hierarchical key
The 50 GB physical-partition limit; add more RU/s
The 10,000 RU/s limit; enable autoscale

questions.explanation: A logical partition (all items sharing one key value) cannot exceed 20 GB, so 35 GB requires a key that keeps any single value below the ceiling — for example a synthetic or hierarchical key.
questions.domain_title: Design and implement data models

questions.stem: Which partition key makes loading one user's recent posts a cheap single-partition read while still spreading load evenly across the user base?

options.text: /postId, a random GUID assigned per post
/createdDate, today's date
/userId
/status

questions.explanation: /userId has high cardinality, spreads load across many users, and appears as the equality filter for the hottest query, so a user's posts land in one logical partition. A random GUID forces fan-out, a date creates a write hotspot, and status is low-cardinality.
questions.domain_title: Design and implement data models

questions.stem: Which of the following are partition key anti-patterns? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: A low-cardinality field such as /status
/customerId when it is high-cardinality and the common query filter
A monotonically increasing value such as today's /eventDate
A random GUID that never appears in any query filter

questions.explanation: Low-cardinality keys create hot partitions, a monotonically increasing key concentrates current writes on one partition, and a GUID never used in filters forces every query to fan out. A high-cardinality key aligned to the common filter (customerId) is the good choice.
questions.domain_title: Design and implement data models

questions.stem: A funds transfer must debit one account and credit another atomically. What partition-key constraint does this impose?

options.text: Both account items must share the same partition key value
The two accounts must be in different logical partitions
A cross-partition transaction must be enabled on the account
Multi-region writes must be turned on

questions.explanation: Transactional batches and server-side scripts operate within a single logical partition only, so items that must change atomically must share the same partition key value. There are no cross-partition transactions.
questions.domain_title: Design and implement data models

questions.stem: A container is partitioned by /customerId. Which query is cheaper in RUs?

options.text: SELECT * FROM c WHERE c.status = "open"
SELECT * FROM c WHERE c.customerId = "C-1001" AND c.status = "open"
Both cost the same
Neither can use the index

questions.explanation: Including the partition key in the filter makes the query single-partition, which is the cheapest read. Filtering by status alone omits the partition key and fans out to every physical partition, raising RU cost and latency.
questions.domain_title: Design and implement data models

questions.stem: A container provisioned at 40,000 RU/s throttles requests to one popular product while overall usage is low. What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: The account has run out of storage
A hot partition — that key value receives disproportionate requests and exceeds its per-partition RU slice
The consistency level is set too weak
The index on the product name is missing

questions.explanation: Provisioned RU/s is divided evenly across physical partitions, so concentrating requests on one key value can trigger 429s even while total RU/s has headroom. The remedy is to repartition (for example a synthetic or hierarchical key) to spread the load.
questions.domain_title: Design and implement data models

questions.stem: Devices all report under a handful of tenant IDs, causing hot partitions. Which synthetic partition key improves distribution while keeping tenant+device queries targeted?

options.text: Partition by /tenantId alone
Concatenate tenantId and deviceId (e.g., tenantId-deviceId) and store it as the partition key
Partition by a random GUID
Partition by the ingestion timestamp

questions.explanation: Concatenating tenant and device into one stored property raises cardinality and spreads writes, and queries that supply both components stay targeted. The synthetic value must be a real, stored property to partition on it.
questions.domain_title: Design and implement data models

questions.stem: A multi-tenant app has a few huge tenants that break the 20 GB limit, but most queries filter by tenant and then by user. Which partitioning solves both problems?

options.text: A synthetic key with a random suffix on tenantId
A hierarchical partition key /tenantId then /userId
Partition by /userId only
A single container partitioned by /country

questions.explanation: A hierarchical (subpartitioned) key lets a first-level value exceed 20 GB, while queries supplying the leading prefix (tenant, or tenant+user) still route to a small subset of partitions. A random suffix would scatter a tenant's data across buckets.
questions.domain_title: Design and implement data models

questions.stem: Orders are queried by customerId and, separately, by productId, both needing single-partition speed. How do you serve both efficiently?

options.text: Keep one container by /customerId and rely on cross-partition queries for product lookups
Add a unique key on productId
Enable multi-region writes so both queries are fast
Keep the primary container by /customerId and maintain a second container by /productId, synced via the change feed

questions.explanation: A container has exactly one partition key definition, so a differently-partitioned second copy (kept in sync with the change feed) serves the other access path with cheap single-partition reads.
questions.domain_title: Design and implement data models

questions.stem: Roughly how many RUs does a 1-KB point read consume, and what increases that number?

options.text: About 1 RU; larger items, more indexed paths, complex queries, and stronger consistency increase it
About 10 RU regardless of item size or consistency
About 1 RU, and nothing increases it
0 RU, because point reads are free

questions.explanation: A 1-KB point read is the anchor cost of about 1 RU. RU cost rises with item size, the number of indexed properties, query complexity, and stronger (strong/bounded) consistency, which roughly doubles read cost.
questions.domain_title: Design and implement data models

questions.stem: A dev/test workload has bursty, unpredictable traffic in a single region and you don't want to manage RU/s. Which capacity mode fits best?

options.text: Provisioned manual throughput
Provisioned autoscale
Serverless
Database-level shared throughput

questions.explanation: Serverless bills per consumed RU with nothing to provision, which suits spiky, intermittent, single-region workloads like dev/test. Provisioned modes require reserving RU/s and target sustained traffic.
questions.domain_title: Design and implement data models

questions.stem: Which statements about Cosmos DB capacity models are true? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: Serverless is available in a single region only
Free tier waives the first 1000 RU/s and 25 GB per account
A provisioned container has a minimum of 400 RU/s
Serverless supports multi-region writes

questions.explanation: Serverless is single-region and pay-per-use, free tier gives the first 1000 RU/s + 25 GB per account, and provisioned containers have a 400 RU/s minimum. Global distribution and multi-region writes require provisioned throughput.
questions.domain_title: Design and implement data models

questions.stem: You have 20 rarely-used containers and want to minimize cost without a strict per-container SLA. Which throughput model fits?

options.text: Dedicated container-level throughput on each container
Database-level (shared) provisioned throughput
Serverless on each container
A high autoscale maximum on every container

questions.explanation: Database-level shared throughput spreads one RU/s pool across up to 25 containers economically, at the cost of no per-container guarantee (noisy-neighbor risk). Dedicated throughput is the choice when predictable performance is required.
questions.domain_title: Design and implement data models

questions.stem: A container provisioned at 10,000 RU/s is replicated to three regions. Roughly what is the throughput cost relative to a single region?

options.text: The same as one region
About 2×
About 3× (each region provisions 10,000 RU/s)
About one-third

questions.explanation: Throughput is provisioned per region, so provisioned cost is roughly RU/s × number of regions. Enabling multi-region writes would add still more cost.
questions.domain_title: Design and implement data models

questions.stem: In the Azure portal, where do you change a container's RU/s and pick manual vs. autoscale?

options.text: The Keys blade
The Firewall and virtual networks blade
Data Explorer under the container's Scale & Settings (or the Scale blade)
The Replicate data globally blade

questions.explanation: Throughput mode (Manual or Autoscale) and RU/s are configured in Data Explorer under Scale & Settings (or the Scale blade); you can switch between modes later (with a cooldown).
questions.domain_title: Design and implement data models

questions.stem: A corporate firewall allows only outbound port 443. Which SDK connection mode must the application use?

options.text: Direct mode
Gateway mode
Either mode works equally well
Direct mode over TCP ports 10000–20000

questions.explanation: Gateway mode routes all requests over HTTPS on port 443, making it firewall-friendly. Direct mode needs a range of TCP ports that the firewall blocks, so it is unavailable here.
questions.domain_title: Design and implement data models

questions.stem: What is the recommended, key-free way to authenticate a CosmosClient to the data plane?

options.text: Embed the account primary key in configuration
Use a connection string that includes the account key
Use a Microsoft Entra ID credential (e.g., DefaultAzureCredential) with Cosmos DB data-plane RBAC
Generate a shared access signature per request

questions.explanation: A Microsoft Entra ID TokenCredential avoids storing account keys and enables least-privilege, RBAC-based data-plane access — the preferred approach over keys or connection strings.
questions.domain_title: Design and implement data models

questions.stem: A team wants to run integration tests with no Azure resources or cost. What tool enables this, and what is a key limitation?

options.text: The Azure Cosmos DB emulator at https://localhost:8081/, which is single-region with no global distribution and uses a fixed local key/endpoint
A free-tier account, which is limited to one region
A serverless account, which cannot be used in CI
A production account restricted to RBAC only

questions.explanation: The emulator reproduces the NoSQL API locally for free at a fixed endpoint and well-known key, so tests run offline. Its main limitation is single-region operation with no global distribution or multi-write; never use its well-known key in production.
questions.domain_title: Design and implement data models

questions.stem: Which HTTP status codes from Cosmos DB are transient and retriable? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: 429 (request rate too large)
404 (not found)
503 (service unavailable)
408 (timeout)
409 (conflict)

questions.explanation: 429, 449, 503, and 408 are transient/retriable and largely handled by the SDK. 404 and 409 are non-retriable client errors (not found and conflict).
questions.domain_title: Design and implement data models

questions.stem: An app creates a new CosmosClient on every request and suffers latency spikes and socket exhaustion. What is the fix?

options.text: Create a client per request but dispose it promptly
Use a single, long-lived CosmosClient (a DI singleton) reused for all requests
Switch the client to Gateway mode
Lower MaxItemCount on all queries

questions.explanation: CosmosClient is thread-safe and caches connections and routing metadata, so one long-lived instance should be reused. Creating one per request causes socket/SNAT exhaustion and cold caches on every call.
questions.domain_title: Design and implement data models

questions.stem: In the Cosmos DB NoSQL query language, what does JOIN t IN c.tags actually do?

options.text: Joins each item to a matching item in another container
Unwinds the tags array within each item (an intra-item self-join)
Performs a cross-partition join across documents
Joins the container to another database

questions.explanation: JOIN in Cosmos DB is a self-join within a single item that unwinds (flattens) an array. There are no cross-document or cross-container joins.
questions.domain_title: Design and implement data models

questions.stem: Which built-in functions help you query schema-flexible arrays and property types? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: ARRAY_CONTAINS
IS_DEFINED
IS_STRING
CROSS_JOIN

questions.explanation: ARRAY_CONTAINS is an array function and IS_DEFINED/IS_STRING are type-checking functions for schema-flexible data. CROSS_JOIN is not a Cosmos DB function.
questions.domain_title: Design and implement data models

questions.stem: You need items that have any tag with weight > 10, without exploding the array through a JOIN cross-product. Which construct is best?

options.text: A GROUP BY over the tags
A correlated subquery with EXISTS over c.tags
A DISTINCT projection
An ORDER BY on tag weight

questions.explanation: A correlated subquery with EXISTS evaluates the array condition once per item without producing the full JOIN cross-product, lowering RU cost on large arrays.
questions.domain_title: Design and implement data models

questions.stem: A case-insensitive search using WHERE LOWER(c.name) = @n is expensive. What design keeps it index-served?

options.text: Add a composite index on name
Store a precomputed lowercase property (e.g., nameLower) and filter on it directly
Use UPPER instead of LOWER
Increase the container's provisioned RU/s

questions.explanation: Wrapping the filtered property in a function like LOWER() defeats the index and forces a scan. Storing a normalized lowercase copy and filtering on it keeps the query index-served.
questions.domain_title: Design and implement data models

questions.stem: Why should user-supplied filter values be passed via QueryDefinition.WithParameter rather than string-concatenated into the query text?

options.text: It reduces network latency between the app and the service
It is the only way to run a query with a WHERE clause
It prevents query injection and enables query-plan reuse
It automatically strengthens the read consistency level

questions.explanation: Parameterized queries bind values safely instead of interpolating raw input, preventing injection and allowing query-plan reuse. It has no effect on consistency.
questions.domain_title: Design and implement data models

questions.stem: You already have an item's id and its partition key. Should you use ReadItemAsync or a SELECT * FROM c WHERE c.id = @id query?

options.text: The query, because it can use the index
ReadItemAsync (a point read), because it is ~1 RU and lowest latency
Either — they cost the same
A stored procedure, to reduce RU cost

questions.explanation: A point read by id + partition key bypasses the query engine and is the cheapest, lowest-latency operation (~1 RU for ~1 KB). Even a single-item query invokes the engine and costs more.
questions.domain_title: Design and implement data models

questions.stem: Which statements about the SDK point-operation methods are correct? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: CreateItemAsync fails with 409 if the id already exists in the partition
ReplaceItemAsync requires the id to already exist
UpsertItemAsync creates the item if absent or replaces it if present
CreateItemAsync overwrites an existing item with the same id

questions.explanation: Create inserts and returns 409 on a conflicting id, Replace overwrites an existing item, and Upsert does whichever applies. Create never overwrites an existing item.
questions.domain_title: Design and implement data models

questions.stem: You need to flip a single field on a large document without transferring the whole item back and forth. Which operation is ideal?

options.text: ReplaceItemAsync after reading the full item
UpsertItemAsync with the full item
A patch (partial document update) with a Set operation
A stored procedure that reads then rewrites the item

questions.explanation: A patch sends only the changed path to the server, avoiding a full read-modify-write and reducing RU and bandwidth. It also avoids the round-trip race that a read-then-replace introduces.
questions.domain_title: Design and implement data models

questions.stem: The service must update an order and its inventory record atomically, and both items share a partition key value. Which SDK feature guarantees all-or-nothing?

options.text: Bulk execution with AllowBulkExecution = true
A TransactionalBatch executed on that partition key
Two separate UpsertItemAsync calls
Propagating a change-feed event to the inventory record

questions.explanation: A transactional batch groups the operations into one all-or-nothing transaction within a single logical partition, so the shared partition key value makes it possible. Bulk is high-throughput but not atomic, and separate calls are not transactional.
questions.domain_title: Design and implement data models

questions.stem: Which constraints apply to a Cosmos DB TransactionalBatch? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: All operations must target the same partition key value
It is bounded to 100 operations
It is bounded to a 2 MB payload
It can span multiple logical partitions

questions.explanation: A transactional batch is atomic within a single logical partition and is limited to 100 operations or 2 MB. There is no cross-partition transactional batch.
questions.domain_title: Design and implement data models

questions.stem: You need to import millions of documents as fast as possible, and atomicity across them is not required. Which SDK feature fits?

options.text: TransactionalBatch
Bulk execution (AllowBulkExecution = true) with many concurrent operations
One-by-one CreateItemAsync calls awaited sequentially
A stored procedure loop over all items

questions.explanation: Bulk execution maximizes write throughput by grouping concurrent operations per physical partition; it is not transactional. Temporarily raising RU/s during the load helps avoid 429s.
questions.domain_title: Design and implement data models

questions.stem: Two clients read the same item and both try to update it. How do you prevent the second write from silently overwriting the first, without locking?

options.text: Acquire a pessimistic lock on the item before updating
Switch the account to Strong consistency
Pass the read _etag in IfMatchEtag; the stale writer gets HTTP 412 and must re-read and retry
Add a unique key on the item's version field

questions.explanation: ETag-based optimistic concurrency compares the item's _etag; if it changed, the write fails with HTTP 412 (Precondition Failed), so the caller re-reads and retries. This is lock-free and prevents lost updates.
questions.domain_title: Design and implement data models

questions.stem: A per-request ConsistencyLevel override can set an individual read to a stronger level than the account's default consistency.
questions.explanation: A per-request override can only relax (weaken) the account default, never strengthen it. Weakening to session or eventual reduces RU cost and latency for reads that tolerate slightly stale data.

options.text: True
False

questions.domain_title: Design and implement data models

questions.stem: Which statements about HTTP 429 responses from Cosmos DB are true? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: It means the operation exceeded the provisioned RU/s ("request rate too large")
The response includes an x-ms-retry-after-ms hint
Fixes include increasing RU/s (or autoscale) and reducing hot-partition skew
It indicates a code bug and is never retriable

questions.explanation: 429 is RU throttling, not a bug; it carries a retry-after hint the SDK honors, and the real fix is adding RU/s or spreading load off a hot partition. It is retriable.
questions.domain_title: Design and implement data models

questions.stem: When you execute a stored procedure, what must you always provide, and why?

options.text: An index policy, so the procedure can query efficiently
A partition key value, because the procedure runs against a single logical partition
A continuation token to resume prior execution
An _etag for concurrency control

questions.explanation: Stored procedures execute against a single logical partition, so the runtime requires a partition key value at execution to scope the operation. All of the procedure's work runs in one implicit, partition-scoped transaction.
questions.domain_title: Design and implement data models

questions.stem: How is a user-defined function (UDF) invoked, and why is filtering a WHERE clause on a UDF a performance concern?

options.text: It is called inside a query with the udf. prefix; filtering on it cannot use the index, so the query scans
It is called as an SDK point operation and is fully index-served
It runs automatically on every write and blocks the operation
It is invoked from a stored procedure and reads data directly

questions.explanation: A UDF is a scalar JavaScript function callable only from within a query via the udf. prefix; it has no data access and is not index-backed, so filtering on it forces a scan. Prefer UDFs in projections rather than filters when performance matters.
questions.domain_title: Design and implement data models

questions.stem: A team runs a serverless Azure Cosmos DB account and now needs it to serve low-latency reads from three continents while surviving a regional outage. What must change first before any additional regions can be associated?

options.text: Enable availability zones on the existing serverless account
Convert the account to provisioned (or autoscale) throughput
Redeploy the application with a multi-region connection string
Add the new regions during a scheduled maintenance window

questions.explanation: Serverless accounts are single-region only, so global distribution is impossible until the account uses provisioned throughput; only then can additional regions be associated. Adding regions itself needs no redeploy or downtime.
questions.domain_title: Design and implement data distribution

questions.stem: A single-write-region account has service-managed failover enabled but no failover priority order configured. The write region goes down. What happens?

options.text: The lowest-priority read region is promoted to write automatically
All read regions are promoted to write simultaneously
No region is promoted automatically, so a manual forced failover is required
The original write region auto-resumes as soon as it recovers

questions.explanation: Automatic promotion needs both the feature enabled and a ranked failover priority list; with no ranked target, the reliable fix during the outage is a manual forced failover to a healthy region.
questions.domain_title: Design and implement data distribution

questions.stem: During a normal maintenance window with all regions healthy, an operations team wants to move the write region from East US to West Europe with no data loss. Which operation fits?

options.text: Forced (customer-managed) failover of the write region
Service-managed failover
Change write region (planned failover)
Disable service-managed failover and delete the East US region

questions.explanation: With all regions healthy, change write region lets replication catch up before promoting West Europe, so there is no data loss; a forced failover would risk losing unreplicated writes and planned failover cannot run during an actual outage.
questions.domain_title: Design and implement data distribution

questions.stem: Which sequence lists the five Azure Cosmos DB consistency levels correctly from strongest to weakest?

options.text: Strong, Session, Bounded staleness, Consistent prefix, Eventual
Strong, Bounded staleness, Session, Consistent prefix, Eventual
Bounded staleness, Strong, Session, Eventual, Consistent prefix
Strong, Bounded staleness, Consistent prefix, Session, Eventual

questions.explanation: The defined order from strongest to weakest is Strong, Bounded staleness, Session, Consistent prefix, Eventual, with Session as the account default.
questions.domain_title: Design and implement data distribution

questions.stem: A social app must ensure each user always sees their own most recent post immediately, while other users can tolerate slight delays. Which consistency level is the best fit?

options.text: Eventual
Consistent prefix
Bounded staleness
Session

questions.explanation: Session consistency guarantees read-your-writes within the user's own session via the session token, so the author always sees their new post while keeping low latency and high throughput for everyone else; it is also the account default.
questions.domain_title: Design and implement data distribution

questions.stem: Which statements about the impact of consistency level on request unit cost and availability are correct? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: Strong and Bounded staleness reads cost roughly 2x the RUs of the weaker levels
Write RU cost is identical across all consistency levels for a given operation
Strong consistency is supported together with multi-region writes
Weaker levels such as Session, Consistent prefix, and Eventual offer higher availability and throughput
Strong reads consume the same RUs as Eventual reads

questions.explanation: Strong and Bounded staleness reads use a two-replica local quorum (~2x RUs), write RU cost does not vary by level, and weaker levels give higher availability and throughput. Strong is not supported with multi-region writes, and Strong reads cost more than Eventual reads.
questions.domain_title: Design and implement data distribution

questions.stem: In which scenario does Azure Cosmos DB fail to meet the usual sub-10 ms P99 latency guarantee?

options.text: Session reads served from a single region
Eventual writes replicated across two regions
Strong writes that must commit across multiple regions
Bounded staleness reads served from a single region

questions.explanation: Only Strong multi-region writes break the sub-10 ms P99 guarantee, because the write must commit in every region first; its latency is roughly 2x RTT + 10 ms between the farthest regions. Reads stay under 10 ms P99 at every level.
questions.domain_title: Design and implement data distribution

questions.stem: Which statements accurately describe multi-region writes (active-active) in Azure Cosmos DB? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: Every associated region can accept writes (active-active)
The topology provides 99.999% read and write availability
Strong consistency can be enabled to guarantee correctness
Concurrent writes to the same item in different regions can conflict
Bounded staleness is the recommended consistency level

questions.explanation: Multi-region writes make every region writable, deliver 99.999% read+write availability, and allow cross-region conflicts that require a conflict-resolution policy. Strong consistency is not supported and Bounded staleness is an anti-pattern with multi-region writes.
questions.domain_title: Design and implement data distribution

questions.stem: A new API for NoSQL container must resolve concurrent cross-region updates by keeping the record with the highest application-supplied version number, using no server-side code. Which configuration fits?

options.text: Custom policy with a registered merge stored procedure
Last Write Wins using the default _ts timestamp
Last Write Wins with a custom numeric conflict-resolution path such as /version
Change the existing container's policy to LWW after creation

questions.explanation: In Last Write Wins you can name a custom numeric conflict-resolution path where the highest value wins, with no stored procedure. It must be set at container creation because the policy is immutable afterward.
questions.domain_title: Design and implement data distribution

questions.stem: Which single-account configuration best meets Tailwind Global's requirements?

options.text: Single write region with Strong consistency and service-managed failover
Multi-region writes with Session consistency and a Last Write Wins conflict-resolution policy
Multi-region writes with Strong consistency and a custom merge stored procedure
Single write region with Bounded staleness and an ordered preferred-region list

questions.explanation: Only multi-region writes give lowest local read and write latency in every region plus survival of a regional outage; Session is a supported level for active-active, and Last Write Wins resolves the acceptable cross-region conflicts deterministically. Strong consistency is incompatible with multi-region writes.
questions.domain_title: Design and implement data distribution

questions.stem: A team needs near real-time Power BI reporting over an Azure Cosmos DB for NoSQL container, but the production workload cannot tolerate any additional Request Unit pressure or ETL to maintain. Which integration should they configure?

options.text: Read the transactional store from Synapse Spark with the cosmos.oltp format
Azure Cosmos DB mirroring for Microsoft Fabric
An Azure Data Factory copy pipeline on a nightly schedule
An Azure Functions change-feed trigger writing to Blob Storage

questions.explanation: Mirroring continuously replicates the database into OneLake as Delta/Parquet in near real-time with no RU consumption and no transactional impact, and Power BI reads it in Direct Lake mode — no ETL pipeline to build. See Mirroring Azure Cosmos DB in Microsoft Fabric.
questions.domain_title: Integrate an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: An engineer tries to enable Fabric mirroring for an Azure Cosmos DB for NoSQL account that has a single write region, but the option is unavailable. Which prerequisite is most likely missing?

options.text: The analytical store must be enabled on every container first
Azure Synapse Link must be turned on at the account level
Continuous backup (7-day or 30-day) must be enabled on the account
A second write region must be added to the account

questions.explanation: Mirroring requires continuous backup on the account; enabling 7-day continuous backup (which is free) satisfies the prerequisite. Mirroring does not depend on the analytical store or Synapse Link, and multiple write regions are actually unsupported.
questions.domain_title: Integrate an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: Which statements about the Azure Cosmos DB Spark connector (versus Fabric mirroring) are true? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: Reading the transactional store with the cosmos.oltp format consumes Request Units.
Mirroring, unlike the Spark connector, can write results back into Cosmos DB.
The Spark connector can write data back to a Cosmos DB container.
The cosmos.olap format reads the analytical store read-only and RU-free.

questions.explanation: The cosmos.oltp format reads/writes the transactional store and consumes RUs, and only the Spark connector (not mirroring) can write back; cosmos.olap reads the analytical store RU-free and read-only. Mirroring is one-way and read-only, so option b is false.
questions.domain_title: Integrate an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: When enabling the analytical store on a container, which Analytical TTL value keeps all history indefinitely?

options.text: A positive number of seconds, such as 86400

questions.explanation: Analytical TTL of -1 means infinite retention (keep all history), a positive n expires analytical data after n seconds, and 0 disables the analytical store. See What is Azure Cosmos DB analytical store?.
questions.domain_title: Integrate an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: Which Azure Synapse serverless SQL pool construct queries the Azure Cosmos DB analytical store over Synapse Link without impacting the transactional workload?

options.text: spark.read.format("cosmos.oltp")
OPENROWSET with PROVIDER = 'CosmosDB'
A linked-server query with OPENQUERY
BULK INSERT from the container endpoint

questions.explanation: Serverless SQL reads the isolated analytical store with OPENROWSET(PROVIDER = 'CosmosDB', ...), so there is no RU cost and no transactional impact. The cosmos.oltp format is a Spark option that reads the transactional store and consumes RUs.
questions.domain_title: Integrate an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: A Spark job must read the most up-to-the-second state of a container using spark.read.format("cosmos.oltp"). What is the key trade-off of this approach?

options.text: It can only read data that is older than two minutes
It cannot be used for streaming or write-back
It reads the transactional store and consumes provisioned RUs, affecting OLTP performance
It requires the analytical store to be disabled first

questions.explanation: The cosmos.oltp format reads the transactional store directly, returning live data but consuming RUs and potentially affecting transactional performance. When a scenario stresses no transactional impact, the analytical store (cosmos.olap) is the intended answer.
questions.domain_title: Integrate an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: You need an incremental feed that includes deletes and TTL removals, writes to a data lake through no-code mapping data flows, and does not consume Request Units. Which capability fits?

options.text: Change data capture in the Azure Cosmos DB analytical store
The transactional change feed in latest-version mode
A custom _ts-based incremental query against the container
Fabric mirroring to OneLake

questions.explanation: Analytical-store CDC natively captures deletes and TTL operations, runs RU-free off the analytical store through ADF/Synapse mapping data flows, and manages GLSN-based checkpoints internally for exactly-once delivery. Latest-version change feed does not emit deletes.
questions.domain_title: Integrate an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: In a Microsoft Fabric Warehouse using time travel with OPTION (FOR TIMESTAMP AS OF '...'), what is the default data retention window within which you can query the past?

options.text: 7 days
30 days
90 days
120 days

questions.explanation: Fabric Warehouse time-travel retention defaults to 30 days and is configurable from 1 to 120 days; a query to a point in time outside the window fails. See Configurable data retention in Fabric Data Warehouse.
questions.domain_title: Integrate an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: Which serverless design lets the downstream service react to every new or changed item in near real time and fan those events out to multiple decoupled consumers?

options.text: Enable the analytical store and query it from Synapse serverless SQL
Mirror the database to OneLake and read it with Power BI Direct Lake
Schedule an Azure Data Factory copy pipeline every five minutes
An Azure Functions Cosmos DB trigger that publishes each change to Azure Event Hubs

questions.explanation: The Azure Functions Cosmos DB trigger reads the change feed with automatic checkpointing and scaling (via a lease container), and an Event Hubs output binding fans each event out to multiple decoupled consumers in near real time. Mirroring and the analytical store serve the historical-analytics need, not low-latency reaction.
questions.domain_title: Integrate an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: When configuring an Azure AI Search indexer over Azure Cosmos DB for NoSQL, which configurations are required to incrementally pick up new/changed documents and to remove deleted ones from the index? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: A HighWaterMarkChangeDetectionPolicy on the _ts column
A SoftDeleteColumnDeletionDetectionPolicy using a Boolean flag such as isDeleted
A GROUP BY clause in the data-source query to deduplicate results
Hard-delete detection that automatically removes purged items

questions.explanation: Incremental indexing relies on the HighWaterMarkChangeDetectionPolicy on _ts, and only soft-delete detection is supported (hard deletes are not detected). GROUP BY/DISTINCT in the data-source query are unsupported because they break continuation-token pagination.
questions.domain_title: Integrate an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: A write-heavy container automatically indexes several large text fields that are never queried, and write RU charges are high. What is the least-effort change that lowers write cost while still auto-indexing any new properties added later?

options.text: Increase the container's provisioned RU/s.
Set the indexing mode to none.
Keep the included path /* and add the unqueried fields to the excluded paths.
Exclude /* and include only /id.

questions.explanation: Keeping /* included lets new properties auto-index, while explicitly excluding the unqueried paths stops indexing them and reduces write RUs. Setting mode none would disable queries and TTL.
questions.domain_title: Optimize an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: An engineer removes a property path from a container's indexing policy. When does the removal take effect?

options.text: Immediately.
After the asynchronous index transformation completes.
Only after the container is restarted.
After the next write touches each affected item.

questions.explanation: Index removals take effect right away; only index additions require an asynchronous, online transformation to finish before the new index is used.
questions.domain_title: Optimize an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: Which statements about the none indexing mode are correct? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: It disables all queries on the container.
It blocks setting TTL on the container.
It is the recommended mode for read-heavy reporting containers.
It speeds up bulk ingestion by removing secondary-index write overhead.
New containers can enable lazy indexing as an alternative to it.

questions.explanation: Mode none turns a container into a pure key/value store: queries are disabled and TTL cannot be set, but it makes bulk loads faster by dropping index maintenance. New containers cannot use lazy indexing.
questions.domain_title: Optimize an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: A query SELECT * FROM c WHERE c.status = 'active' runs against a container partitioned by /tenantId and is expensive. Which single change most reduces its RU cost?

options.text: Raise the provisioned RU/s on the container.
Add an ORDER BY clause to the query.
Wrap the filter in LOWER(c.status).
Add a c.tenantId = @id predicate so the query targets a single partition.

questions.explanation: Filtering on the partition key converts a cross-partition fan-out into a single-partition query, sharply lowering RU cost and latency. Higher RU/s only lets you run more queries before rate limiting.
questions.domain_title: Optimize an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: Which query characteristic prevents index use and forces a full scan?

options.text: Filtering on the partition key.
Performing a point read by id and partition key.
Applying LOWER() to a property in the WHERE clause.
Projecting a single property in the SELECT list.

questions.explanation: System functions that transform a property, such as LOWER() and UPPER(), defeat the index and cause a full scan that retrieves many documents to return few.
questions.domain_title: Optimize an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: Which statements about query cost and query execution metrics are correct? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: A point read by id and partition key is the cheapest read operation.
A low IndexHitRatio with RetrievedDocumentCount much larger than OutputDocumentCount indicates a scan.
Raising provisioned RU/s lowers an individual query's request charge.
Cross-partition queries fan out to every physical partition and merge results client-side.

questions.explanation: Point reads are the cheapest operation, a large retrieved-vs-output gap with a low IndexHitRatio signals a scan, and cross-partition queries execute on every partition and merge client-side. Higher RU/s does not reduce a query's per-operation charge.
questions.domain_title: Optimize an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: Using the .NET v3 SDK, which response member returns the RU cost of a point read?

options.text: response.ActivityId
response.RequestCharge
response.ETag
response.Diagnostics

questions.explanation: Every .NET v3 response exposes RequestCharge as a double holding the RUs consumed by that operation. The underlying HTTP header is x-ms-request-charge.
questions.domain_title: Optimize an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: For a paged query, the RequestCharge reported on a single page equals the total RU cost of the entire query.
questions.explanation: A query can span multiple pages, so you must sum RequestCharge across every page to get the total cost; one page's charge is not the whole cost.

options.text: True
False

questions.domain_title: Optimize an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: A read-heavy app connects through a dedicated gateway in gateway mode but still incurs RU charges on repeated identical point reads. The account uses bounded staleness consistency. What explains this?

options.text: The integrated cache serves only session or eventual reads, so bounded staleness bypasses it.
Gateway connection mode disables the integrated cache.
The item cache never caches point reads.
The integrated cache requires strong consistency to return 0-RU hits.

questions.explanation: The integrated cache only serves reads at session or eventual consistency; strong, bounded staleness, and consistent prefix bypass it. Switch the request or account to session or eventual to get 0-RU cache hits.
questions.domain_title: Optimize an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: An app can tolerate data up to 10 minutes old and wants to maximize integrated-cache hits. What should it configure?

options.text: Lower MaxIntegratedCacheStaleness to 0.
Set MaxIntegratedCacheStaleness to 10 minutes.
Switch the account to strong consistency.
Use direct connection mode.

questions.explanation: Raising MaxIntegratedCacheStaleness lets more repeated reads be served from cache before entries are treated as stale, increasing the hit rate. A value of 0 would force fresh reads and reduce hits.
questions.domain_title: Optimize an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: Which conditions are required to use the Azure Cosmos DB integrated cache? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: A provisioned dedicated gateway.
Gateway connection mode.
Session or eventual consistency.
Strong consistency.
Direct connection mode.

questions.explanation: The integrated cache lives in the dedicated gateway and needs the dedicated-gateway connection, gateway connection mode, and session or eventual consistency. Strong consistency and direct mode bypass the cache.
questions.domain_title: Optimize an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: A team wants to run code on every insert and update to a container with zero worker infrastructure and automatic scaling. What should they use?

options.text: A change feed pull-model loop.
A stored procedure.
A materialized view.
An Azure Functions Cosmos DB trigger.

questions.explanation: The Azure Functions Cosmos DB trigger wraps the change feed processor to deliver serverless, auto-scaling change-feed processing without managing any hosts.
questions.domain_title: Optimize an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: An Azure Functions Cosmos DB trigger fails to start because no lease container exists. What is the simplest fix in the trigger binding?

options.text: Manually pre-create a lease container with a /category partition key.
Set CreateLeaseContainerIfNotExists = true in the binding.
Switch the trigger to all-versions-and-deletes mode.
Enable continuous backup on the account.

questions.explanation: Setting CreateLeaseContainerIfNotExists = true auto-creates the lease container (with a /id partition key) so processing can begin.
questions.domain_title: Optimize an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: Which statements about the change feed processor are correct? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: It requires both a monitored container and a lease container.
Delivery is at-least-once, so the delegate should be idempotent.
You scale out by starting instances that share the processor name and lease container but use different instance names.
It is provided as the change feed processor in the Python and Node.js SDKs.

questions.explanation: The processor reads from the monitored container and checkpoints in the lease container, delivers changes at least once, and rebalances leases when you add instances with the same processor name. Python and Node.js use the pull model instead.
questions.domain_title: Optimize an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: A Python developer wants to consume the change feed from within their application using the SDK. Which model must they use?

options.text: The change feed pull model.
The change feed processor.
An Azure Functions trigger is the only supported option.
The change feed is not available in Python.

questions.explanation: The change feed processor is a .NET v3 and Java v4 feature; Python and Node.js consume the change feed via the pull model with manual continuation tokens.
questions.domain_title: Optimize an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: The change feed estimator for a processor reports steadily increasing lag. What action does this suggest?

options.text: Reduce the number of processor instances.
Increase MaxIntegratedCacheStaleness.
Scale out by adding more processor instances.
Switch to all-versions-and-deletes mode.

questions.explanation: Growing lag means changes arrive faster than they are processed; adding instances distributes leases across hosts and increases throughput.
questions.domain_title: Optimize an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: You use the change feed to copy data into a read-optimized container that has a different partition key. Which statement is true?

options.text: Propagation through the change feed is strongly consistent.
Replication is eventually consistent, and the destination partition key should suit the target query.
Ordering is guaranteed across the entire destination container.
The destination container must reuse the source partition key.

questions.explanation: Change-feed replication is eventually consistent, and you choose the destination partition key for the target query. Order is guaranteed only within a single partition key value.
questions.domain_title: Optimize an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: When a customer is deleted, all their orders must be archived, but the change feed in latest version mode never delivers deletes. How do you trigger the cascade?

options.text: Add a foreign-key constraint on the orders container.
Switch the account to strong consistency.
Poll the customers container on a timer to find deletions.
Soft-delete the customer with a deleted:true flag so the update flows through the change feed.

questions.explanation: Latest version mode omits hard deletes, so modeling the delete as a soft-delete update lets the consumer detect the flag and cascade the archival. Cosmos DB has no server-side foreign keys.
questions.domain_title: Optimize an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: A leaderboard is maintained by incrementing per-player totals in a summary container from the change feed. Why must the increment logic be idempotent?

options.text: Because the change feed guarantees exactly-once delivery.
Because delivery is at-least-once, so a reprocessed event could double-count.
Because point reads of the summary document cost RUs.
Because a composite index is required to increment values.

questions.explanation: The change feed delivers at least once, so an event may be processed more than once; without idempotency a naive increment would double-count and corrupt the persisted aggregate.
questions.domain_title: Optimize an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: A container grows without bound, but only the last 90 days are queried. How do you keep it lean while retaining history cheaply?

options.text: Increase the provisioned RU/s on the container.
Set the indexing mode to none.
Use the change feed to copy aged items to Azure Blob Storage and set TTL to expire them from Cosmos DB.
Add composite indexes for the history queries.

questions.explanation: Tiering with the change feed copies aged records to cheap Blob Storage, and TTL then expires the live copies automatically, shrinking RU and storage cost on the operational container.
questions.domain_title: Optimize an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: A telemetry container ingests millions of writes per hour and is queried only occasionally on two fields. Which indexing strategy minimizes write cost?

options.text: Keep the default policy that indexes /*.
Exclude /* and include only the two queried paths.
Set the indexing mode to none.
Add composite indexes on every property.

questions.explanation: Indexing only the two paths you actually query minimizes per-write index maintenance on a high-ingest workload. Mode none would break the occasional queries and block TTL.
questions.domain_title: Optimize an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: An app runs proximity searches over GPS coordinates, but the queries are slow and unindexed. Which index kind is needed?

options.text: A range index.
A composite index.
A spatial index, which is not created by default.
A tuple index.

questions.explanation: Geospatial queries over Point, Polygon, MultiPolygon, and LineString require a spatial index, which is not part of the default policy and must be added explicitly.
questions.domain_title: Optimize an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: Which techniques help optimize indexing for the least write overhead while keeping queries fast? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: Keep /* included and exclude large unqueried subtrees.
Add composite indexes for multi-property ORDER BY and multi-filter queries.
Add the new index before removing the old one when swapping indexes.
Leave PopulateIndexMetrics enabled permanently in production.

questions.explanation: Excluding unqueried subtrees lowers write RUs, composite indexes serve real query shapes, and add-before-remove avoids a coverage gap because removals are instant while additions require a transformation. PopulateIndexMetrics adds overhead and is for debugging only.
questions.domain_title: Optimize an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: The hottest operation loads a single user's most recent posts by filtering on userId and sorting by createdAt descending, and the team wants cheap, predictable reads. Which single change best serves this query?

options.text: Add a composite index on (userId ASC, createdAt DESC) to serve the filter-and-sort.
Set the indexing mode to none on the posts container.
Remove all indexes to lower write cost.
Wrap createdAt in LOWER() in the query.

questions.explanation: A query that filters on one property and orders by another needs a composite index whose path sequence and sort orders match; (userId ASC, createdAt DESC) serves loading a user's recent posts efficiently and predictably.
questions.domain_title: Optimize an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: During traffic spikes, an app's telemetry shows a rising count of HTTP 429 responses from Azure Cosmos DB. What does a 429 indicate, and what is the appropriate first response?

options.text: The request rate exceeded the provisioned RU/s budget on a partition; increase throughput (or enable autoscale) and/or fix a hot partition key.
The request was unauthorized; rotate the account keys.
A transient write conflict occurred; permanently abandon the operation.
The container was not found; recreate it.

questions.explanation: A 429 means the per-second RU budget for a physical partition was exhausted; the SDK auto-retries, but sustained 429s call for more RU/s or a better partition key.
questions.domain_title: Maintain an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: Normalized RU Consumption is pinned at 100% and clients receive 429s, yet the account's total RU usage is far below its provisioned throughput. What is the most likely cause?

options.text: The account is under-provisioned overall.
A network outage between regions.
A single hot partition, because the metric is the maximum utilization across all partition key ranges.
TTL is deleting documents too quickly.

questions.explanation: Normalized RU Consumption is a maximum (not an average) across partition key ranges, so one overloaded partition can pin it at 100% while total account usage stays low.
questions.domain_title: Maintain an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: Clients observe about 120 ms request latency, but the Azure Monitor server-side latency metric reports about 4 ms for the same operations. Where should you focus your investigation?

options.text: The Cosmos DB indexing policy.
The network, SDK configuration, or client resource pressure, because server-side latency excludes network round-trip.
The backup policy.
The partition key storage size.

questions.explanation: The server-side latency metric measures time inside the service and excludes network time, so a large client-vs-server gap points to the network, gateway mode, or client-side pressure.
questions.domain_title: Maintain an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: You must be notified automatically whenever the account performs a regional failover. Which monitoring construct is the best fit?

options.text: A metric alert on Normalized RU Consumption.
A log alert on DataPlaneRequests.
An alert on the "Region failed over" activity/metric signal.
A CORS configuration change notification.

questions.explanation: A regional failover is surfaced as an activity/metric event that alerting can watch, unlike RU or request-level data-plane signals.
questions.domain_title: Maintain an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: Which Azure Cosmos DB alerting scenarios are best implemented as activity-log alerts rather than metric alerts? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: Account keys were rotated.
Requests are being throttled (429).
A region failed over.
Normalized RU Consumption exceeds 90%.

questions.explanation: Key rotation and region failover are control-plane events surfaced in the activity log, whereas throttling and RU utilization are metric-based alerts.
questions.domain_title: Maintain an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: True or false: Platform metrics for an Azure Cosmos DB account are collected automatically, but resource logs must be explicitly enabled with a diagnostic setting before you can query them.
questions.explanation: Metrics flow automatically at one-minute granularity, whereas resource logs require a diagnostic setting routing them to Log Analytics, a storage account, or Event Hubs.

options.text: True
False

questions.domain_title: Maintain an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: You suspect one tenant's logical partition key is consuming most of your container's RUs. Which resource log category confirms which keys burn the most throughput?

options.text: PartitionKeyStatistics
ControlPlaneRequests
QueryRuntimeStatistics
PartitionKeyRUConsumption

questions.explanation: PartitionKeyRUConsumption aggregates RU charge per logical partition key, exposing hot keys; PartitionKeyStatistics reports storage instead.
questions.domain_title: Maintain an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: Which resource log category reports the storage size of your largest logical partition keys so you can detect a key approaching its hard limit, and what is that limit?

options.text: PartitionKeyRUConsumption; 10 GB
PartitionKeyStatistics; 20 GB
DataPlaneRequests; 50 GB
ControlPlaneRequests; 20 GB

questions.explanation: PartitionKeyStatistics reports per-logical-partition storage size, and a logical partition is capped at 20 GB, after which further writes for that key fail.
questions.domain_title: Maintain an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: Your security team needs an audit trail of every throughput change, firewall/network change, and key operation on the account. Which resource log category should you enable?

options.text: DataPlaneRequests
PartitionKeyStatistics
ControlPlaneRequests
QueryRuntimeStatistics

questions.explanation: ControlPlaneRequests audits management operations such as throughput, firewall/network, and key changes; DataPlaneRequests audits document operations.
questions.domain_title: Maintain an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: Auditors require the ability to restore data to the exact second before an accidental delete, using self-service with no support ticket. Which backup mode must the account use?

options.text: Periodic backup with the default 4-hour interval.
Continuous backup with point-in-time restore.
Periodic backup with locally redundant storage.
Manual export to Blob Storage via Data Factory.

questions.explanation: Only continuous backup offers self-service point-in-time restore to any second within the 7- or 30-day window; periodic-mode restores require a Microsoft support ticket.
questions.domain_title: Maintain an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: After a periodic-mode restore into a new account, which of the following are NOT carried over and must be reconfigured? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: Account keys (new ones are generated).
The documents that existed at the backup time.
Firewall, VNet, and private-endpoint configuration.
RBAC role assignments and stored procedures/triggers/UDFs.

questions.explanation: A restore recreates the data into a new single-region account but does not bring back keys, network rules, RBAC assignments, or server-side scripts.
questions.domain_title: Maintain an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: True or false: After migrating an Azure Cosmos DB account from periodic to continuous backup, you can later switch it back to periodic backup.
questions.explanation: The periodic-to-continuous migration is one-way and cannot be reversed.

options.text: True
False

questions.domain_title: Maintain an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: Before initiating a point-in-time restore, you want to confirm the most recent moment you can restore to for a container. What should you retrieve?

options.text: The latest restorable timestamp for the container.
The account's provisioned RU/s.
The container's indexing policy version.
The Normalized RU Consumption value.

questions.explanation: The latest restorable timestamp defines the most recent valid restore point within the retention window; the event feed helps locate a moment just before a destructive change.
questions.domain_title: Maintain an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: After a continuous-backup restore, documents with a short TTL disappear almost immediately from the new account. How do you preserve them on the next restore?

options.text: Increase the RU/s before restoring.
Restore into the same source account in place.
Restore with --disable-ttl True.
Enable analytical store on the container.

questions.explanation: TTL is restored by default, so aged documents expire on arrival unless you pass --disable-ttl True during the restore.
questions.domain_title: Maintain an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: A compliance policy requires your organization to control and be able to revoke the encryption key protecting data at rest. Which option satisfies this?

options.text: Disable encryption at rest and manage it in the application.
Service-managed keys.
Always Encrypted deterministic fields.
Customer-managed keys (CMK) stored in Azure Key Vault.

questions.explanation: Encryption at rest is always on; CMK adds a customer-controlled key layer in Key Vault with rotation and revocation control, which service-managed keys do not provide.
questions.domain_title: Maintain an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: Which mechanisms can you use to restrict from where requests are allowed to reach an Azure Cosmos DB account? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: IP firewall (ipRules / CIDR ranges).
VNet service endpoints for specific subnets.
Private endpoints (Private Link).
A container-level CORS allowedOrigins list.
An Always Encrypted encryption policy.

questions.explanation: The IP firewall, VNet service endpoints, and Private Link private endpoints all control network reachability; CORS governs browser origins and Always Encrypted protects fields, not network access.
questions.domain_title: Maintain an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: With customer-managed keys (CMK) enabled, which statement is true about what the customer key protects?

options.text: It double-encrypts the main transactional store, but container names and partition key values (metadata) are not encrypted with the CMK.
It encrypts all metadata, including container and stored-procedure names.
It double-encrypts the Synapse Link analytical store.
It replaces the automatic AES-256 encryption at rest.

questions.explanation: CMK double-encrypts stored transactional data, but metadata such as names, index paths, and partition key values — plus the analytical store and continuous backups — fall outside CMK.
questions.domain_title: Maintain an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: You need a team to create databases and change throughput on an account but never read account keys, connection strings, or documents. Which built-in role should you assign?

options.text: Cosmos DB Built-in Data Contributor
Cosmos DB Operator
Owner
Cosmos DB Built-in Data Reader

questions.explanation: The control-plane Cosmos DB Operator role manages accounts and resources while explicitly blocking access to keys, connection strings, and data.
questions.domain_title: Maintain an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: A user with the Reader role can browse containers in Data Explorer, but with account keys disabled they get errors when reading items. What is missing?

options.text: A CORS allowedOrigins entry.
A private endpoint.
A data-plane RBAC role assignment (for example, Data Reader).
The Cosmos DB Operator role.

questions.explanation: Control-plane RBAC lets a user see resources in Data Explorer, but reading or writing data without keys additionally requires a data-plane RBAC assignment.
questions.domain_title: Maintain an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: The application must read and write documents with no account keys anywhere in code, using least-privilege access. Which combination meets this requirement?

options.text: Store the primary key in Key Vault and read it with a managed identity.
Assign the Cosmos DB Operator role to the app.
Assign the Cosmos DB Built-in Data Contributor role to the app's Entra identity and set disableLocalAuth = true.
Enable CORS and use a wildcard origin.

questions.explanation: Data-plane RBAC grants keyless read/write through Microsoft Entra ID, and disableLocalAuth = true eliminates key-based access entirely.
questions.domain_title: Maintain an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: A single-page app at https://app.contoso.com is blocked by a CORS error when calling the API for NoSQL. Which statement about resolving this is correct?

options.text: CORS is unavailable for Cosmos DB; use a backend proxy only.
Add the exact origin to the account's CORS allowedOrigins list; per-subdomain wildcards like https://*.contoso.com are not supported.
CORS applies to all Cosmos DB APIs equally.
Set publicNetworkAccess = Disabled to fix the CORS error.

questions.explanation: CORS is supported for the API for NoSQL; you list exact origins (or *), but sub-domain wildcard patterns are not accepted.
questions.domain_title: Maintain an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: Account keys are still required by a legacy component, but they must never appear in source code or configuration. What is the recommended pattern?

options.text: Store the key as an Azure Key Vault secret and retrieve it at runtime with a managed identity.
Hard-code the key but obfuscate it.
Email the key to the operations team.
Store the key in an app setting in plain text.

questions.explanation: Storing keys as Key Vault secrets fetched via managed identity keeps secrets out of code/config and centralizes rotation; disabling keys and using Entra ID is stronger still.
questions.domain_title: Maintain an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: You are enabling customer-managed keys (CMK) for a new Cosmos DB account. Which of the following are required for the setup to succeed? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: Azure Key Vault must have Soft Delete enabled.
Azure Key Vault must have Purge Protection enabled.
Cosmos DB must be granted Get / WrapKey / UnwrapKey on the key.
The account must use the API for Cassandra.
Encryption at rest must first be manually turned on.

questions.explanation: CMK requires a Key Vault with Soft Delete and Purge Protection, and the Cosmos DB principal must have Get/WrapKey/UnwrapKey (or the Key Vault Crypto Service Encryption User role).
questions.domain_title: Maintain an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: You must encrypt an SSN field with Always Encrypted while still supporting equality (exact-match) queries on it. Which encryption type do you choose?

options.text: Randomized
Deterministic
Service-managed key
Transparent data encryption

questions.explanation: Deterministic encryption produces identical ciphertext for identical plaintext, enabling equality filters; randomized is stronger but not queryable.
questions.domain_title: Maintain an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: Which data-movement tools are appropriate for continuous, streaming ingestion into or out of Azure Cosmos DB? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: Apache Kafka connector.
Azure Stream Analytics output sink.
Azure IoT Hub custom endpoint routing.
SDK bulk import (AllowBulkExecution).
Azure Data Factory scheduled Copy activity.

questions.explanation: Kafka, Stream Analytics, and IoT Hub routing handle streaming scenarios; SDK bulk suits one-off high-volume imports and Data Factory suits scheduled no-code ETL.
questions.domain_title: Maintain an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: You must import millions of documents from custom .NET code as fast as possible. How do you enable bulk mode, and when is the setting fixed?

options.text: Set AllowBulkExecution = true per request; it can change at any time.
Call a BulkImport() method on the container.
Set AllowBulkExecution = true in CosmosClientOptions at client creation; it is immutable afterward.
Enable it in the account's ARM template.

questions.explanation: AllowBulkExecution = true is set on CosmosClientOptions at construction and can't change later; the SDK then batches concurrent operations per partition.
questions.domain_title: Maintain an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: You need a scheduled, no-code export of Cosmos DB data to Azure Blob Storage, and later to also load data back in. What is true of the Azure Data Factory/Synapse Cosmos DB connector?

options.text: It works as both a source and a sink, supporting scheduled copy in and out.
It can only read from Cosmos DB, never write.
It only supports streaming, not scheduled copies.
It requires custom code for each direction.

questions.explanation: The connector is bidirectional (source and sink) and supports no-code, schedulable copy activities in and out of Cosmos DB.
questions.domain_title: Maintain an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: Which statements about the Azure Cosmos DB Kafka Connect connectors are correct? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: The sink connector writes Kafka topic records into Cosmos DB.
The source connector reads the Cosmos DB change feed and publishes to Kafka.
The containers.topicmap setting maps each topic to a container using topic#container.
The source connector writes into Cosmos DB using the transactional store.

questions.explanation: Sink means Kafka to Cosmos DB; source reads the change feed to Kafka; containers.topicmap pairs topics with containers.
questions.domain_title: Maintain an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: A Stream Analytics job cannot write to Cosmos DB because of the account's IP firewall. What is the correct fix?

options.text: Add the job's static outbound IP to the firewall.
Enable "Accept connections from within Azure datacenters" (0.0.0.0), because Stream Analytics has no fixed IP.
Disable public network access on the account.
Switch the account to periodic backup.

questions.explanation: Stream Analytics has no static IP to allowlist, so you permit Azure-internal traffic; the sink then upserts documents by id.
questions.domain_title: Maintain an Azure Cosmos DB solution

questions.stem: You want to run heavy Spark analytics over Cosmos DB data without consuming transactional RUs, then write curated results back into a container. Which Spark data source formats do you use?
